title: Probability Monolith
description: A tall faceted marker recording the likelihood of an unrealized outcome. The monolith is a dark asymmetrical slab split by one vertical probability aperture, surrounded by smaller calibrated cuts, a buried luminous core, and a thin hovering capstone that never quite settles.
narrativeFunction: Probability monoliths create distant architectural depth and a readable field of alternate outcomes. Their apertures can display changing likelihood, align into routes, frame destination nodes, and provide a severe geometric counterpoint to the unlived garden.

orientation.originMeaning: The origin sits at the center of the planted base plane. The hovering capstone, aperture animation, and field-alignment socket move relative to this fixed foundation.

silhouette.primary: A tall narrow slab leaning slightly to one side, wider through the lower third, and ending in a broken angled crown below a detached capstone.
silhouette.secondary: One vertical aperture cuts through most of the height and divides the slab into unequal left and right masses.
silhouette.tertiary: Probability ticks, edge notches, recessed glyphs, hairline luminous fractures, calibration inserts, and eroded base fragments provide close detail around the aperture.
silhouette.negativeSpace: The vertical aperture remains genuine open space or a deep transmissive volume. The air gap beneath the capstone forms the second essential void.
silhouette.recognitionTest: At distance the asset reads as a severe probability marker with one tall luminous cut and floating crown, not as a tombstone, ordinary obelisk, rectangular pillar, or portal frame.

materials.surfaceDescription: Dense dark violet stone with broad faceted planes, chipped lower edges, geological banding, and fine cracks bending around the aperture.
Deep cyan transmissive light with moving density bands, brighter probability ticks, a darker center, and visible depth rather than a flat glowing stripe.
Dark violet alloy with sharp bevels, engraved decimal marks, pale emission along its lower hovering face, and small magnetic alignment pins.

components.detail: Cut the crown on an angle, taper one side inward, facet the front into three unequal planes, and model a wider lower third with chipped footing corners.

components.role: Tall luminous probability channel running through the slab.
components.detail: The channel widens near the upper third, narrows toward the base, contains layered depth, and either passes through the stone or sits deeply recessed.

components.role: Calibration marks stepping along one edge of the aperture.
components.detail: Tick lengths encode a nonlinear probability sequence, use irregular spacing, and alternate between engraved metal and small emissive indicators.

components.role: Detached angled crown suspended above the broken slab.
components.detail: The capstone mirrors part of the broken crown profile, includes two alignment pins, and remains visibly separated by a twelve-centimeter air gap.

components.role: Small faceted stone fragments and probability debris near the footing.
components.detail: Fragments cluster on the leaning side, appear chipped from the same geological material, and leave the route-alignment socket unobstructed.

assemblyOrder: Cut or insert full-height probability aperture
Model broken crown and detached capstone
Scatter base fragments and secondary markings
deformationRules: The whole slab leans one to four degrees while its base remains planted.
Front and side faces use broad intentional facets rather than noise displacement.
Aperture width may change forty percent over height but never closes completely.
variationRules: Monolith height ranges from sixty-five to one hundred forty percent across a field.
Aperture hue and fill level encode local probability while staying inside the cyan-violet family.
Capstone rotation and gap height vary, but every instance retains a detached crown.

topology.target: Game-ready hard-surface stone and metal topology with a separate capstone, deep aperture volume, replaceable calibration ticks, and scalable field instancing.
topology.bevel: Use broad stone bevels on the major slab, tighter capstone and tick bevels, and crisp aperture edges catching cyan light.
topology.subdivision: Avoid global subdivision. Preserve broad facets and use local smoothing only on aperture glass and selected eroded base transitions.
topology.normals: Weighted normals maintain calm slab planes and clean capstone highlights while modeled facets provide deliberate shading breaks.
topology.uv: Use triplanar stone mapping, unique aperture density coordinates, reusable calibration trim UVs, and an emission mask for cracks and tick states.

animation.motionCharacter: Stone remains still while probability moves inside it. The aperture fills and drains slowly, ticks answer in sequence, and the capstone floats only a few centimeters.
constraints: The capstone air gap never closes.
The aperture remains visually continuous from lower to upper termination.

collision.description: Use one slightly tapered box collider covering the main slab only. Capstone, ticks, aperture effects, and base fragments remain excluded.

sockets.purpose: Field placement at the planted base center.
Camera or interaction focus at the probability channel midpoint.
Optional path or lattice alignment marker on the forward side.

orthographicGuidance.front: Show unequal slab halves divided by the vertical aperture, leaning outline, broken angled crown, and persistent capstone gap.
orthographicGuidance.side: Reveal slab depth, planted footing, capstone offset, and whether the aperture is a true cut or deep transmissive channel.
orthographicGuidance.top: Make asymmetrical front facets, slight rotation, off-center aperture placement, and route socket direction readable.
orthographicGuidance.threeQuarter: Use low grazing light to emphasize broad stone facets, cyan aperture depth, engraved ticks, and the detached crown.
orthographicGuidance.scaleCue: Place a human beside the base; the default monolith stands approximately three times human height.

validation: The vertical aperture remains the dominant internal feature from fifty meters.
The slab halves are visibly unequal and use a nonrectangular crown profile.
The capstone remains detached by a persistent visible air gap.
Probability ticks use nonuniform spacing and at least three lengths.
Field variations preserve family recognition despite height and lean changes.
LOD2 retains leaning slab, aperture cut, and detached crown.
Ground and focus sockets remain aligned after whole-instance scaling.
